episode_title: Briefing Chat: Are scientists funny? The evidence is in — and it's no joke
summary: This Nature Briefing podcast episode covers two distinct science stories: research demonstrating a causal link between gut microbiome composition and cognitive aging in mice through the bacterium Parabacteroides goldsteinii, and a study analyzing humor attempts at academic biology conferences, finding that two-thirds of jokes fell flat. The microbiome research suggests potential therapeutic targets for age-related cognitive decline, while the humor study provides evidence-based tips for scientists to effectively incorporate humor into presentations.
science_topics: Gut-brain axis and vagus nerve signaling
Medium-chain fatty acids and immune response
Phage therapy for targeted bacterial elimination
Object recognition memory testing in mice
Humor in academic communication and learning enhancement
claims_to_note: Gut microbiome shifts in young mice cohabiting with old mice resulted in cognitive decline comparable to aged mice - claimed as causal evidence rather than merely correlative
Implantation of P. goldsteinii bacterium in young mice worsened object recognition memory
Antibiotic elimination or phage-targeted therapy of P. goldsteinii improved cognitive performance in old mice to levels comparable with young mice
Medium-chain fatty acids produced by P. goldsteinii activate immune response that suppresses vagus nerve activity
Two-thirds of humor attempts in academic talks fell flat; only 9% achieved strong laughter from entire audience
Research papers with humorous titles show citation advantage
Laughter can enhance learning and boost audience attention
policy_relevance: Potential therapeutic applications of phage therapy targeting specific gut bacteria for age-related cognitive decline
Dietary interventions as preventive approach for cognitive aging
key_quotes: This is potential causal evidence... the researchers think it might be because of what this bacterium produces, which is lots and lots of medium chain fatty acids. This in turn activates an immune response, which seems to suppress activity along the vagus nerve, which is that big main nerve that sends signals back and forth between your gut and your brain.

Victoria Stout says that even subdued laughter shouldn't be considered a failure, saying that it can help people relax. And sometimes humor can be a welcome change to the energy in the room, help refocus the mind.
Scientists have had incredibly interesting lives and humour comes from the reality of our lived experience. Therefore, you are funny.

Strategies to Improve Cardiac Arrest Survival: A Time to Act
publications.description: Cardiac arrest often strikes seemingly healthy individuals without warning and without regard to age, gender, race, or health status. Representing the third leading cause of death in the United States, cardiac arrest is defined as "a severe malfunction or cessation of the electrical and mechanical activity of the heart ... [which] results in almost instantaneous loss of consciousness and collapse". Although the exact number of cardiac arrests is unknown, conservative estimates suggest that approximately 600,000 individuals experience a cardiac arrest in the United States each year. In June 2015, the Institute of Medicine (IOM) released its consensus report Strategies to Improve Cardiac Arrest Survival: A Time to Act, which evaluated the factors affecting resuscitation research and outcomes in the United States. Strengthening Post-Hurricane Supply Chain Resilience: Observations from Hurricanes Harvey, Irma, and Maria
publications.description: Resilient supply chains are crucial to maintaining the consistent delivery of goods and services to the American people. The modern economy has made supply chains more interconnected than ever, while also expanding both their range and fragility. In the third quarter of 2017, Hurricanes Harvey, Irma and Maria revealed some significant vulnerabilities in the national and regional supply chains of Texas, Florida, the U.S. Virgin Islands, and Puerto Rico. The broad impacts and quick succession of these three hurricanes also shed light on the effectiveness of the nation's disaster logistics efforts during response through recovery. Drawing on lessons learned during the 2017 hurricanes, this report explores future strategies to improve supply chain management in disaster situations. publications.committee: Committee on Building Adaptable and Resilient Supply Chains After Hurricanes Harvey, Irma, and Maria
